title: Deprecate BoltDB, preventing creation of new databases.
body:
This is one of the breaking changes in Podman 5.0: removing the ability to create new instances of the old Bolt database. This does not remove support for the database entirely, as existing Bolt databases will still be usable, but all new installs will use SQLite after this point - if Bolt is forced by config, we'll just error.

We don't have plans to outright remove the Bolt code. If that were to happen, it'd be Podman 6.0 at least, and a significant enough change it'd warrant a lot of discussion and planning. We do intend to start winding down support of BoltDB, though, and new features may be added only to SQLite from here on.

I have added an escape hatch via an undocumented environment variable that allows us to continue testing BoltDB in CI (and, if necessary, locally) but I don't want this to be used for any purpose except continued testing of the old DB to ensure we don't break it.

<!--
Thanks for sending a pull request!

Please make sure you've read our contributing guidelines and how to submit a pull request (https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/CONTRIBUTING.md#submitting-pull-requests).

In case you're only changing docs, make sure to prefix the pull-request title with "[CI:DOCS]". That will prevent functional tests from running and save time and energy.

Finally, be sure to sign commits with your real name. Since by opening
a PR you already have commits, you can add signatures if needed with
something like `git commit -s --amend`.
-->

#### Does this PR introduce a user-facing change?

<!--
If no, just write `None` in the release-note block below. If yes, a release note
is required: Enter your extended release note in the block below. If the PR
requires additional action from users switching to the new release, include the
string "action required".

For more information on release notes, please follow the Kubernetes model:
https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/release-notes.md
-->

```release-note
It is no longer possible to create new BoltDB databases; attempting to do so will result in an error. All new Podman installations will now use the SQLite database backend.
```

comments.body: @edsantiago @cevich PTAL
Can we add a test to set the environment to false containers.conf to sqlite and make sure podman fails with no sqlite database?
Now that I think about it, that ought to be possible
I was a little reluctant to incorporate CI details into the PR, but I don't think it's harmful now that I think about it more. Probably easier, even.

I think the `system reset` bit of #20563 is fixed (it's definitely not intended behavior, and if it's not fixed we need to fix it). So, theoretically, we could swap DB in system tests so long as it was used?
If you can make the tests happy, LGTM

@mheon @edsantiago taking a step back here.

    This is one of the breaking changes in Podman 5.0:...snip...all new installs will use 
    SQLite after this point - if Bolt is forced by config, we'll just error.

This is a major version bump, I think we should consider it might be 100% okay to throw away boltdb support entirely AND stop testing it.  What's needed to support this (i.e. a podman 4.x supporting both, such that switching / migrating workloads is possible) is already in place and tested (upgrade tests, eh?).

To summarize: I think maybe we're doing more work here that is required.  In 5.0 let's drop the old baggage and carry on forward.  Eh?  What am I missing?
As an end user, I'm not 100% sure I would be happy if I upgrade and all of a sudden nothing works at all.
Yeah, I don't think we can remove it without a migration strategy, which we don't have time to do at this point. Maybe for 6.0.

I don't mean to belabor my point, but couldn't "migration strategy" be fairly simple documentation:  Export your volumes, secrets, and network setup.  Copy to new install of podma (different machine) or safely wipe existing + new setup w/ SQLite.  Re-import volumes, secrets, and network setup.  Done.

No?

(My intention here is to try and simplify our life.  Since whatever's in 5.0 CI will stick around fora potentially VERY long time.  Let's make our life easier if we can)
That might work for Fedora, but this has to go into RHEL 9, so we have to keep Bolt around for the lifespan of RHEL9 feature releases.

"this has to go into RHEL 9" - Oh right, damn.

`/me` thought he remembered something about NOT doing major version bumps in RHEL like this.  I think the need for this PR illustrates why :disappointed:  Grumble...grumble...grumble...okay I'll shut my mouth now :man_shrugging:

Well, [yuk](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5393091192946688/html/int-podman-debian-13-rootless-host-sqlite.log.html#t--Podman-Info-podman-db-backend-info-basic-check--1). It's because of the way e2e tests are set up, where every podman command has an enormous `--this --that --whatever` set of explicit options. One of those is `--db-backend`, so the command that's being run is actually:
```
$ podman [options] --log-level=info info --format {{.Host.DatabaseBackend}}
          ^^^^^^^-> which includes --db-backend sqlite
```
...whereas before it was
```
--db-backend sqlite [crap crap] --db-backend ""
```
...which I guess is not the same thing.

I still think deleting this entire test is the best thing to do, but if you want to put back the `""` code I guess that'll work. It's just not really a test of anything.

Weird. in [f38 rootless](https://api.cirrus-ci.com/v1/artifact/task/5393550720892928/html/sys-podman-fedora-38-root-host-boltdb.log.html#t--00273):
```
<+056ms> # # podman image scp foo.bar/nonesuch/c_iypxcy1e6m:mytag some25515dude@localhost::
...
         # Error: the BoltDB backend has been deprecated, no new BoltDB databases can be created: invalid argument
```
This test podman-scps an image from root to rootless or vice-versa. I can't figure out where the destination is getting the idea that it should initialize as boltdb. I will look closer tomorrow.
Changes LGTM once @edsantiago is happy with the tests.  At the moment, the CI is hiccuping, I'm not sure if it's related.

> where the destination is getting the idea that it should initialize as boltdb. I will look closer tomorrow.

Ed, I haven't been watching this PR very closely.  But if the "idea" is passed around in an env. var., then care should be taken to ensure it's mentioned in [one of the special lists](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/cirrus/lib.sh#L98-L104) (example).

Yep, close, but I don't think it's the list thing. And it's not `/etc/cirrus_environment` because the desired envariable is already there. I need to figure out something such that:
```
# ssh rootless@localhost cmd
```
...loads the desired environment. Maybe mucking with the user's .bashrc, or system bashrc, I dunno, almost meeting time so I'm giving up for now.
It is possible that this will pass CI, but if so it's because I'm doing manual muckery in a Rerun-terminal-etc window. DO NOT MERGE.

> loads the desired environment

[ssh re-exec's](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/cirrus/runner.sh#L527-L529) the `runner.sh` script which loads `lib.sh`
`lib.sh` then [loads `ci_environment`](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/contrib/cirrus/lib.sh#L36-L37)
To clarify: `/etc/cirrus_environment` != `/etc/ci_environment` != `$CIRRUS_ENV`.  `/etc/ci_environment` is the preferred mechanism (in this CI) for making variables persist across execution contexts.

Somehow podman is invoking ssh in a way that the remote end does not source `rc` files. I can't figure how it does this, and can't reproduce a command-line-ssh way to say "don't source rc files". 

Anyhow, giving up. I suggest just
```bash
    if [[ "$CI_DESIRED_DATABASE" = "boltdb" ]]; then
        skip "this is a nightmare"
    fi
```

The pull request process is described [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/owners.md#the-code-review-process)
Needs approval from an approver in each of these files: - ~~[OWNERS](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/OWNERS)~~ [edsantiago,mheon] Approvers can indicate their approval by writing `/approve` in a comment Approvers can cancel approval by writing `/approve cancel` in a comment

/lgtm
/unhold

/hold

@mheon Looks like you have a lot of don't merge tags?

Blush. That was me, when I was playing with the live CI VM and thought the job might pass. It would've been very very bad if that had merged. Sorry for forgetting to clear those. I've cleared them, and mergebot should notice soon.
